revision.text:
[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762}} [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox Shinto shrine | Name = Toyosaka Shrine | Image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | Address = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | Coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | Festival Deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | Founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | Annual Festival = October 1 | Main Festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== Deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox Shinto shrine | Name = Noda Shrine | Image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | Address = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | Coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | Festival Deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | Founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | Annual Festival = March 5 | Main Festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== Deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Special Government Shrines,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

== References == <references group="" responsive="1"></references> [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]][[category:original pages]] [[en:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]][[Category:1873 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[ja:豊榮神社・野田神社]] [[simple:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]]

== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | address = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | annual festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.

[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762}} [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | address = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | annual festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | address = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | annual festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.

Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | address = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | annual festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | address = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | annual festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Special Government Shrines,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.

{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762}} [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | festival deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.

{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Special Government Shrines,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Special Government Shrines,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]

[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762}} [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.

[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762}} [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}}
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} [[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[Aki Province]] to [[Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969)
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Special Government Shrines,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>
== Noda Shrine ==
{{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{Interlanguage link|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref> {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} [[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Toyosaka Shrine == *
== Noda Shrine == *
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[JR West]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Special Government Shrines,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
