revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:26, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>[[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] to [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[:en:Hagi Castle|Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:26, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>[[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] to [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[:en:Hagi Castle|Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:26, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>[[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] to [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[:en:Hagi Castle|Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]

In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270

The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:26, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Toyosaka and Noda Shrines]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} are two [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>[[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] to [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[:en:Hagi Castle|Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270

In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]

[[File:140720_Noda-jinja_Yamaguchi_Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The entrance to the compound where both shrines are located.]]
{{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} are two [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}.
== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>[[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] to [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[:en:Hagi Castle|Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]

== Toyosaka Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Toyosaka Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Toyosaka Shrine bridge and entrance.jpg|deity=[[Mōri Motonari]]|consecration_year=1762| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Toyosaka Shrine | image = [[File:140720 Toyosaka-jinja Yamaguchi Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-1 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|19.7|N|131|28|50.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Toyosaka Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = Mōri Motonari | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1762 (Hōreki 12) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = October 1 | Main festival = Goshōsai (July 16) }} Toyosaka Shrine is a Shinto shrine in Yamaguchi, located next to Noda Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourist Information {{!}} Toyosaka-jinjya Shinto Shrine {{!}}30{{!}}|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/signqr/details/en/30.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref>[[File:Mori Motonari.jpg|180px|thumb|Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Motonari, who was a powerful feudal lord in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and laid the foundation for the Mōri clan of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]].
=== History === In 1600 (Keichō 5), following the defeat in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], Mōri Terumoto moved from [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] to [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]]. He enshrined his grandfather Motonari's spirit at Kasuga Shrine in the village of Ekimukai, Hagi. In 1762 (Hōreki 12), the Chōshū domain lord Mōri Shigetoshi rebuilt the local shrine in the Ni-no-Maru of [[:en:Hagi Castle|Hagi Castle]] and moved Motonari's spirit there, naming it Shōrei Shrine. In 1770 (Meiwa 7), the shrine was renamed Gyōtoku Shrine and Motonari was enshrined as Gyōtoku Daimyōjin.
In 1869 (Meiji 2), Emperor Meiji granted Motonari the divine title of "Toyosaka" due to his reverence for the imperial court. The shrine was moved to the precincts of Taga Shrine in Yamaguchi City and renamed Toyosaka Shrine. The current shrine buildings were constructed in 1872 (Meiji 4), and the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9). In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main shrine building is a three-bay nagare-zukuri style structure. It has a cross-shaped flat layout with an extended roof at the front. In 1997 (Heisei 9), a monument with the inscription "One Heart with Millions" (百万一心) was erected in the grounds, based on the story of Motonari burying a stone with this inscription during the construction of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle.
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties *Hanging scroll portrait of Mōri Motonari (Designated on June 20, 1969) [[File:Mori Motonari painting at Toyosaka Shrine.jpg|right|thumb|Full portrait of Motonari.]]
== Noda Shrine == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=Noda Shrine|religious_affiliation=Shinto|image=Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|deity=[[Mori Takachika]] Mōri Motonori|consecration_year=1873| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Noda Shrine | image = [[File:Inside the Noda Shrine complex.jpg|300px]] | location = 1-1-2 Tenge, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|11|18.7|N|131|28|49.4|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark_scale:10000|name=Noda Shrine}} | deity = Mōri Takachika | Shrine Rank = Former special government shrine, special shrine | founded = 1873 (Meiji 6) | Main Building Style = Nagare-zukuri with copper roofing | festival = March 5 | Main festival = Goshōsai (May 17 for main deity, December 23 for enshrined deities) }} {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Noda Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]], it is located next to {{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|lt=Toyosaka Shrine|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0" /> It is dedicated to Mōri Motonari.<ref>{{Cite web|title=YAMAGUCHI JAPAN TRAVEL GUIDE|url=https://www.visit-jy.com/|access-date=2023-04-07|website=www.visit-jy.com}}</ref>[[File:140720 Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Japan03s.jpg|thumb|Noh theater stage]]
=== deity === The shrine enshrines Mōri Takachika, the last lord of the Chōshū Domain and a key figure in the [[Meiji Restoration]]. It also enshrines his adopted son, Mōri Motonaga.
=== History === In 1873 (Meiji 6), volunteers built a shrine to enshrine Takachika in the grounds of Toyosaka Shrine. The shrine was named Chūsei Shrine after Takachika's posthumous name. In 1874 (Meiji 7), the shrine was renamed Noda Shrine, taking its name from the local area. It was designated a prefectural shrine in 1876 (Meiji 9) and relocated to its current site in 1886 (Meiji 19). In 1915 (Taishō 4), it was elevated to the Rank of special government shrine.
Motonaga was enshrined in 1898 (Meiji 31) as Mōri Motonaga Kō in the subsidiary shrine Hōgien Shrine within the Noda Shrine grounds. In 1936 (Shōwa 11), he was moved to the main hall.
=== Buildings and Grounds === The main buildings are similar in style and layout to those of Toyosaka Shrine. The Noh theater stage within the grounds was donated by Mōri Motonaga’s son, Mōri Motoharu, in 1936 (Shōwa 11). It was designated as a Tangible Cultural Property by Yamaguchi City in 1991 (Heisei 3).
=== Cultural Properties === ;National Important Cultural Properties * Sword (Tachi) signed by Suketomo, with ceremonial mountings (Designated on September 3, 1912) ;Yamaguchi City Tangible Cultural Properties * Noh Theater Stage and auxiliary rooms (Designated on April 23, 1991)
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Toyosaka Shrine''' (豊榮神社) and '''Noda Shrine''' (野田神社) are adjacent Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Yamaguchi City|Yamaguchi City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. Although the shrines are adjacent, with Toyosaka Shrine to the east and Noda Shrine to the west, they are separate religious corporations. They each have their own main halls, worship halls, and corridors, but they share some facilities such as the office. Due to their close relationship, they are explained together here.
Both shrines were formerly designated as special government shrines (別格官幣社) and are currently listed as special shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁). The Yamaguchi Prefecture Shrine Office is also located on the adjacent grounds. These shrines were built in the late Edo period to early Meiji period to enshrine the founders of the Han (feudal domains).
== Access == *About a 15-minute walk from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Yamaguchi Line|Yamaguchi Line]]'s [[:en:Kami-Yamaguchi Station|Kami-Yamaguchi Station]].
== Nearby == * [[Noda Gakuen Junior and Senior High School]] * [[Yamaguchi Saikōtei]] * [[Rurikō-ji]] * [[Dōshū-ji]], Nomura Art Museum * [[:en:Jōei-ji|Jōei-ji]] * [[Yamaguchi Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Ima Hachiman Shrine]]
== Notes == === Toyosaka Shrine in Ōda City === There is another Toyosaka Shrine in [[Ōda City]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], near the [[Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine]]. This shrine also enshrines Mōri Motonari. It was originally a temple called Chōan-ji, founded by Mōri Terumoto in 1571 (Genki 2). In 1866 (Keiō 2), the Chōshū army discovered a wooden statue of Motonari in the temple. The following year, a shrine was built within the temple grounds, and in 1870 (Meiji 3), it was named Toyosaka Shrine, and the temple was abolished.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}} * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Compiled by Niroku Koshinjo, edited by Yamada Yoneki, 'History of Loyalty Deeds: Bekkaku Kanpeishas,' pages 38–41, 92–95, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * 'Great Dictionary of Shinto,' Volume 3, Heibonsha, 1940 * Anzu Yoshihiko and Umeda Yoshihiko, editors and supervisors, 'Shinto Dictionary,' Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, pages 43, 46 * Shirai Eiji and Doki Masanori, editors, 'Shrine Dictionary,' Tokyo-dō Publishing, 1979, pages 252, 269–270
== Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Toyosaka-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Toyosaka Shrine}} {{Commonscat|Noda-jinja (Yamaguchi)|Noda Shrine}} * [[Shizuki-yama Shrine]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

{{ill|Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|ja|豊榮神社・野田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosaka and Noda Shrines|qq=}} are two [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located next to each other in [[:en:Yamaguchi (city)|Yamaguchi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Yamaguchi City Tourism Information Website Nishinokyo Yamaguchi - A strategic base of the Meiji Restoration. - Noda Shrine|url=https://yamaguchi-city.jp/w/en/ishin/spot018.html|access-date=2023-04-07|website=yamaguchi-city.jp}}</ref> Both shrines are dedicated to members of the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mōri clan|qq=}}.
